content.text: AWS Deployments вҖ“ Multi-Project Case Studies

A collection of **production AWS deployments** across multiple projects вҖ” spanning EC2, EKS, Lambda, MSK, EFS, CloudFront, S3, SES, DynamoDB, API Gateway, Route 53, and more. Each project uses AWS differently вҖ” from a serverless auth layer on a homelab-hosted app to a fully cloud-native multi-AZ Kubernetes deployment with managed Kafka.

This isn't a single project вҖ” it's a **portfolio of real AWS architectures** built for actual workloads: a Netflix-style OTT platform, a real-time collaborative document editor, transactional email infrastructure, and static asset delivery at scale.

content.title: AWS Services Used
content.text: EC2 В· EKS В· Lambda В· MSK (Kafka) В· EFS В· S3 В· CloudFront В· SES В· DynamoDB В· API Gateway В· Route 53 В· IAM В· KEDA В· HPA В· Auto Scaling В· Elastic Load Balancer В· VPC
Architecture Philosophy

Every AWS deployment here follows the same core principles вҖ” **use managed services where they add value, avoid them where they add cost without proportional benefit, and always design for failure**.

items: **Serverless for low-traffic, spiky workloads** вҖ” Lambda + API Gateway for auth scales to zero between requests. No always-on server cost for infrequent operations.
**Managed Kafka over self-hosted** вҖ” AWS MSK for SyncDocs provides 3-broker HA with zero operational overhead. Running Kafka yourself on EC2 is a full-time job.
**CloudFront everywhere** вҖ” static assets always behind CloudFront. Zero origin hits for cached content, global edge delivery, free TLS.
**Multi-AZ by default** вҖ” every production deployment spans at least 2 AZs. A single AZ outage should never take down a service.
**Nginx on EC2** вҖ” where a full K8s cluster is overkill, Nginx on EC2 handles reverse proxying, SSL termination, and rate limiting simply and cheaply.
**Self-hosted where cost matters** вҖ” the homelab handles everything that doesn't need AWS scale. AWS handles what does.

content.text: Case Study 1 вҖ“ JoshiOTT (ott.vpjoshi.in)

A **Netflix-style OTT streaming platform** deployed on AWS. The architecture solves three hard problems simultaneously вҖ” efficient HLS video delivery via CloudFront, serverless authentication via Lambda + DynamoDB, and containerized backend scaling via EC2 with Docker Compose behind an Elastic Load Balancer.

content.caption: JoshiOTT AWS architecture вҖ” Route 53 вҶ’ CloudFront вҶ’ ELB вҶ’ Auto Scaling groups across 2 AZs with Lambda auth.

content.text: CloudFront + S3 (OAC)

The React frontend is stored in a **private S3 bucket** вҖ” publicly inaccessible. CloudFront accesses it via **Origin Access Control (OAC)**, AWS's modern replacement for OAI. The bucket policy only allows `GetObject` from the specific CloudFront distribution вҖ” nothing else can read from the bucket.
Lambda Auth + DynamoDB

Authentication is **fully serverless** вҖ” API Gateway triggers a Lambda function on login/signup. Lambda validates credentials against DynamoDB and returns a signed JWT. Scales to zero between requests вҖ” no always-on auth server.

content.code:
POST /auth/login
     в”Ӯ
     в–ј
API Gateway вҶ’ Lambda (Node.js)
     в”Ӯ
     в”ңв”Җв”Җ query DynamoDB: users table
     в”ңв”Җв”Җ bcrypt.compare(password, hash)
     в”ңв”Җв”Җ match вҶ’ jwt.sign({ userId, role }, JWT_SECRET)
     в””в”Җв”Җ return { token }

# Lambda cold start: ~200ms
# Warm invocation: ~30ms
# Cost at 10K logins/month: ~$0.002

content.text: EC2 + Docker Compose + Nginx

The Node.js HLS streaming backend runs on **EC2 instances** in Auto Scaling groups across 2 AZs. Docker Compose manages the frontend + backend containers on each instance. **Nginx** acts as the reverse proxy вҖ” handling SSL termination (for origin вҶ’ ELB traffic), request routing, and rate limiting.

content.title: HLS via CloudFront
content.text: HLS `.m3u8` playlists and `.ts` segment chunks are cached at CloudFront edge after the first request. Subsequent viewers of the same segment hit the CDN cache вҖ” zero origin hits, zero EC2 bandwidth for repeat viewers.
AWS Services вҖ“ JoshiOTT

items: **Route 53** вҖ“ DNS routing for ott.vpjoshi.in to CloudFront.
**CloudFront** вҖ“ CDN for React SPA (S3/OAC) and HLS segment caching.
**S3** вҖ“ Private bucket for React frontend assets, OAC-secured.
**API Gateway** вҖ“ HTTP trigger for Lambda auth handler.
**Lambda** вҖ“ Serverless login/signup, credential validation, JWT issuance.
**DynamoDB** вҖ“ User table вҖ” email, bcrypt password hash, userId.
**Elastic Load Balancer** вҖ“ Distributes traffic across EC2 instances in 2 AZs.
**EC2 + Auto Scaling** вҖ“ Node.js HLS backend with Docker Compose. Scales on CPU.
**Nginx** вҖ“ Reverse proxy on EC2, request routing, rate limiting.
**IAM** вҖ“ Lambda execution role (DynamoDB read), EC2 instance profile.

content.text: Case Study 2 вҖ“ SyncDocs (sync-docs.vpjoshi.in)

The most complex AWS deployment вҖ” a **real-time collaborative document editor** built for scale on Amazon EKS. Uses Operational Transformation for conflict resolution, AWS MSK (Managed Kafka) for decoupled write ingestion, EFS for shared multi-AZ storage, and KEDA for lag-based autoscaling.

content.caption: SyncDocs full AWS architecture вҖ” CloudFront вҶ’ EKS вҶ’ MSK вҶ’ IO Processor вҶ’ EFS across 3 AZs.

content.text: EKS Multi-AZ Setup

All workloads run on **Amazon EKS across 3 AZs** (ap-south-1a/b/c). `topologySpreadConstraints` distributes pods evenly. HPA handles uneven load вҖ” AZ pod counts differ intentionally to reflect real-world asymmetric traffic. MSK sits outside EKS but inside the same VPC.

content.code:
# topologySpreadConstraints вҖ” even pod distribution
spec:
  topologySpreadConstraints:
    - maxSkew: 1
      topologyKey: topology.kubernetes.io/zone
      whenUnsatisfiable: DoNotSchedule
      labelSelector:
        matchLabels:
          app: syncdocs-backend

content.text: AWS MSK вҖ“ Managed Kafka

**AWS MSK** with 3 brokers (one per AZ), replication factor 3. Two topics вҖ” `doc.delta.ops` for document change events and `analytics.events` for usage metrics. Kafka decouples write ingestion from processing вҖ” the backend publishes freely and consumers process at their own pace.

content.code:
# MSK topic config
Topic: doc.delta.ops
  Partitions: 12         # parallelism across IO Processor pods
  Replication Factor: 3  # one replica per AZ broker
  Retention: 24h         # ops older than 24h discarded

Topic: analytics.events
  Partitions: 6
  Replication Factor: 3
  Retention: 7d

content.text: EFS + KEDA Autoscaling

**Amazon EFS** is mounted across all IO Processor pods via the EFS CSI driver with a `ReadWriteMany` PVC вҖ” any pod in any AZ reads and writes the same document files. **KEDA** watches the Kafka consumer group lag and scales IO Processor pods up/down automatically.

content.code:
# KEDA ScaledObject вҖ” scale on Kafka lag
apiVersion: keda.sh/v1alpha1
kind: ScaledObject
metadata:
  name: io-processor-scaler
spec:
  scaleTargetRef:
    name: io-processor
  minReplicaCount: 1
  maxReplicaCount: 10
  triggers:
    - type: kafka
      metadata:
        topic: doc.delta.ops
        bootstrapServers: <msk-endpoint>:9092
        consumerGroup: io-processor-group
        lagThreshold: '100'   # scale up if lag > 100 messages

content.title: Why EFS over S3?
content.text: S3 is eventually consistent for overwrites вҖ” not safe for high-frequency document state updates from multiple pods. **EFS gives POSIX filesystem semantics** with strong consistency. IO Processor pods across all 3 AZs write to the same files without coordination overhead.
AWS Services вҖ“ SyncDocs

items: **CloudFront + S3 (OAC)** вҖ“ React/Vite SPA served from private S3 via CloudFront.
**API Gateway + Lambda + DynamoDB** вҖ“ Serverless auth layer, JWT issuance.
**Amazon EKS** вҖ“ Multi-AZ Kubernetes cluster across ap-south-1a/b/c.
**AWS MSK** вҖ“ Managed Kafka, 3 brokers, RF=3, two topics.
**Amazon EFS** вҖ“ ReadWriteMany shared filesystem via CSI driver.
**KEDA** вҖ“ Kafka lag-based autoscaling for IO Processor and Analytics pods.
**HPA** вҖ“ CPU/memory-based autoscaling for backend API pods.
**VPC** вҖ“ MSK and EKS in the same VPC, no public network hop for Kafka traffic.
**IAM** вҖ“ EKS node roles, MSK access policies, EFS mount permissions.
**Route 53** вҖ“ DNS for sync-docs.vpjoshi.in.

content.text: Case Study 3 вҖ“ Transactional Email via AWS SES

A **serverless transactional email infrastructure** built on AWS SES (Simple Email Service) вҖ” handling both inbound and outbound email for multiple domains. Contact form submissions, automated notifications, and inbound email routing all run through SES with Lambda processing and Cloudflare Email Routing for forwarding.

content.title: Email Addresses
content.text: **Sending** вҖ“ admin@unetify.in (transactional emails, notifications) В· **Receiving** вҖ“ contact@unetify.in (inbound contact form), contact@joshistudio.com (Joshi Studio enquiries) В· All routed via SES + Cloudflare Email Routing.
SES Architecture

content.code:
# OUTBOUND вҖ” sending transactional email
App / Lambda
     в”Ӯ
     в”Ӯ SES SendEmail API
     в–ј

# PROGRAMMATIC INBOUND (SES receipt rules)
Sender вҶ’ contact@joshistudio.com
     в”Ӯ
     в–ј
в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ
в”Ӯ  SES Receipt Rule               в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ  вҶ’ store raw email in S3        в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ  вҶ’ trigger Lambda               в”Ӯ
в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”¬в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ
                 в”Ӯ
                 в–ј
         Lambda processes email
         вҶ’ parse sender, subject, body
         вҶ’ forward / store / notify

content.text: SES Configuration вҖ“ Outbound

SES is configured in **ap-south-2 (Hyderabad)** вҖ” the closest AWS region to India, minimising latency for outbound email. Domain verification, DKIM signing, and SPF records are all configured via DNS (Cloudflare).

content.code:
# DNS records for SES domain verification (Cloudflare)

# SPF вҖ” authorize SES to send on behalf of unetify.in
unetify.in TXT "v=spf1 include:amazonses.com ~all"

# DKIM вҖ” SES-generated CNAME records (3 selectors)
<selector1>._domainkey.unetify.in CNAME <selector1>.dkim.amazonses.com
<selector2>._domainkey.unetify.in CNAME <selector2>.dkim.amazonses.com
<selector3>._domainkey.unetify.in CNAME <selector3>.dkim.amazonses.com

# DMARC вҖ” policy for failed SPF/DKIM
_dmarc.unetify.in TXT "v=DMARC1; p=quarantine; rua=mailto:admin@unetify.in"

content.text: Sending via Lambda

content.title: SES Pricing
content.text: SES costs **$0.10 per 1,000 emails** sent вҖ” essentially free at startup scale. Compared to SendGrid ($19.95/month for 50K emails) or Mailgun ($35/month), SES is orders of magnitude cheaper for transactional volume under 100K/month.

sections.category: Case Study 4 вҖ“ S3 & Static Hosting

content.text: Case Study 4 вҖ“ S3 Static Hosting & Asset Delivery

S3 is used across multiple projects as the **static asset backbone** вҖ” React SPAs, HLS video segments, document assets, and build artifacts all flow through S3 + CloudFront. Each project uses S3 differently depending on its access pattern.

content.code:
# S3 buckets across projects

joshiott-frontend          вҶ’ React SPA (private, OAC)
  в””в”Җв”Җ CloudFront distribution
  в””в”Җв”Җ served at ott.vpjoshi.in

joshiott-hls               вҶ’ HLS video segments (private, signed URLs)
  в””в”Җв”Җ CloudFront with presigned URL policy
  в””в”Җв”Җ .m3u8 playlists + .ts chunks

syncdocs-frontend          вҶ’ React/Vite SPA (private, OAC)
  в””в”Җв”Җ CloudFront distribution
  в””в”Җв”Җ served at sync-docs.vpjoshi.in

syncdocs-uploads           вҶ’ document assets (private)
  в””в”Җв”Җ accessed via presigned GET URLs from EKS pods

ses-inbound                вҶ’ raw inbound email storage
  в””в”Җв”Җ SES receipt rules write here
  в””в”Җв”Җ Lambda reads and processes

content.text: CloudFront Cache Strategy

Cache behavior is configured per path pattern вҖ” SPA index files get short TTLs (must revalidate on deploy), static assets get long TTLs (content-hashed filenames ensure cache busting), and HLS segments get medium TTLs (segment content never changes once written).

content.code:
# CloudFront cache behaviors

/index.html
  Cache-Control: no-cache, no-store
  TTL: 0  вҶҗ always revalidate (new deploy = new HTML)

/assets/*  (JS, CSS вҖ” content-hashed by Vite)
  Cache-Control: public, max-age=31536000, immutable
  TTL: 1 year  вҶҗ safe, filename changes on every build

/stream/*.m3u8  (HLS playlist)
  Cache-Control: public, max-age=300
  TTL: 5 min  вҶҗ short, playlist may update for live streams

/stream/*.ts  (HLS segments)
  Cache-Control: public, max-age=86400
  TTL: 24h  вҶҗ segments are immutable once written

content.title: Cache Invalidation on Deploy
content.text: On every GitHub Actions deploy, a CloudFront invalidation is triggered for `/*` on the frontend distribution вҖ” forcing edge nodes to fetch the new `index.html`. JS/CSS assets don't need invalidation because Vite content-hashes their filenames on every build.
IAM вҖ“ Least Privilege Across All Projects

Every AWS resource uses a **dedicated IAM role with minimal permissions**. No shared roles across projects, no broad `*` policies, no hardcoded access keys in code вҖ” credentials are always injected via environment variables or instance profiles.

content.title: No Hardcoded Credentials
content.text: Zero hardcoded AWS credentials anywhere in the codebase. Lambda functions use execution roles (automatically assumed). EC2 instances use instance profiles. GitHub Actions uses OIDC federation where possible, secrets otherwise. Every access key rotation is a config change, not a code change.
Nginx on EC2 вҖ“ Reverse Proxy Layer

For EC2-based deployments (JoshiOTT), **Nginx** sits between the ELB and the Docker containers as a reverse proxy. It handles request routing to the correct container port, adds security headers, implements rate limiting, and manages graceful connection handling for WebSocket streams.

content.title: Nginx vs ALB
content.text: The ELB (ALB) handles cross-AZ load balancing and health checks. Nginx on each EC2 instance handles per-request routing, headers, and rate limiting. This separation keeps the ALB config simple and puts fine-grained control at the instance level where it's easier to iterate.
AWS Cost Overview
AWS is used deliberately вҖ” only where it provides value that justifies the cost. The homelab handles everything else.

items: **CloudFront + S3** вҖ“ ~$1-3/month per project. Essentially free at startup traffic levels.
**Lambda** вҖ“ Free tier covers ~1M invocations/month. Auth Lambda costs ~$0/month at current usage.
**DynamoDB** вҖ“ Free tier (25GB, 25 WCU/RCU). Costs ~$0/month for user tables.
**SES** вҖ“ $0.10/1000 emails. ~$0-1/month at current volume.
**EC2 (JoshiOTT)** вҖ“ t3.micro on reserved pricing ~$8/month.
**EKS (SyncDocs)** вҖ“ $0.10/hour cluster fee + EC2 nodes. ~$80-120/month at dev scale.
**MSK (SyncDocs)** вҖ“ kafka.t3.small, 3 brokers. ~$50/month.
**Route 53** вҖ“ $0.50/hosted zone/month.

content.title: Homelab vs AWS
content.text: EKS + MSK for SyncDocs is the most expensive setup вҖ” ~$130-170/month. Everything else combined is under $20/month. The homelab saves ~вӮ№10,000/month by self-hosting Grafana, n8n, LLaMA, Postgres, and PocketBase that would otherwise run on AWS.
Full AWS Services Index

content.code:
# Compute
EC2          вҶ’ JoshiOTT backend (Docker Compose, Auto Scaling)
EKS          вҶ’ SyncDocs backend (multi-AZ K8s, 3 AZs)
Lambda       вҶ’ Auth handlers (JoshiOTT, SyncDocs), SES processor

# Storage
S3           вҶ’ Frontend SPAs, HLS segments, SES inbound, build artifacts
EFS          вҶ’ SyncDocs IO Processor shared document storage
DynamoDB     вҶ’ User tables (JoshiOTT, SyncDocs)

# Networking
CloudFront   вҶ’ CDN for all React SPAs and HLS delivery
ELB (ALB)    вҶ’ JoshiOTT load balancing across 2 AZs
API Gateway  вҶ’ HTTP trigger for Lambda auth
Route 53     вҶ’ DNS for ott.vpjoshi.in, sync-docs.vpjoshi.in
VPC          вҶ’ SyncDocs вҖ” EKS + MSK in same private VPC

# Messaging
MSK (Kafka)  вҶ’ SyncDocs: doc.delta.ops + analytics.events
SES          вҶ’ Transactional email (admin@unetify.in)
               Inbound routing (contact@joshistudio.com)

# Scaling
Auto Scaling вҶ’ EC2 groups for JoshiOTT (CPU-based)
HPA          вҶ’ EKS pods for SyncDocs backend (CPU/memory)
KEDA         вҶ’ EKS pods for IO Processor + Analytics (Kafka lag)

# Security & Identity
IAM          вҶ’ Per-service roles, least privilege, no shared credentials
OAC          вҶ’ CloudFront вҶ’ S3 private bucket access

# Regions
ap-south-1   вҶ’ Primary (Mumbai) вҖ” EKS, MSK, EC2, S3, DynamoDB
ap-south-2   вҶ’ SES (Hyderabad) вҖ” lowest latency for Indian email delivery
